coredata.dc:title: Structural aspects, conformational preference and other physico-chemical properties of Artesunate and the formation of self-assembly with graphene quantum dots: A first principle analysis and surface enhancement of Raman activity investigation
coredata.dc:creator: Pooventhiran, T.
Al-Zaqri, Nabil
Alsalme, Ali
Bhattacharyya, Utsab
Thomas, Renjith
coredata.dc:description: Artesunate and its derivatives belong to a family of compounds that are highly effective against Malaria. This drug is active against several other conditions like neurodegerative diseases and cancer. The compound forms a metabolite dihydroartemisinine, which contain an endoperoxide bridge that is stimulated by heme iron leading to oxidative stress, protein and nucleic acid synthesis inhibition, morphologic changes and reduced growth and survival of parasites. This manuscript examines the structure and conformational preferences of the compound and investigates the energy and reactivity descriptors using computational simulations. Studies indicates that the compound forms stable self-assemblies with graphene quantum dots and modified graphene quantum dots, where the more stable complex was formed with the modified system. This self-assembly formation leads to enhancement in Raman activity of the compound after complex formation, hence can be used an analytical tool for the detection of the compound in a mixture. Natural bond orbital analysis examines the various intramolecular electron delocalisations. This drug is found to be a nonlinear optically active compound with respect to standards materials like urea and paranitroacetanilide. We also report the non covalent interactions present in the molecule, reduced density gradient, Lapalcian electron density and local information entropy of the molecule.
coredata.dcterms:subject: DFT
TD-DFT
SERS
NCI
Artesunate

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Structure and conformational preference of the compound
Molecular self-assembly formation of artesunate over graphene and modified graphene quantum dots and enhancement of Raman activities
Nature bonding orbital (NBO) property for Artesunate
Molecular electrostatic potentials (MESP) from electronic and nuclear charges
Average localized ionization energy (ALIE) property for Artesunate
Reduced density gradient (RDG) for Artesunate and Laplacian of electron density for Artesunate

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors extend their appreciation to the Deanship of Scientific Research , King Saud University for funding this work through research group no ( RG-1440-141 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Geometry structure for Artesunate in PBEPBE/aug-cc-cPVDZ level of theory.
Potential energy gradient for Artesunate.
Molecular reactivity descriptors based on FMO's
Molecular electrostatic potentials from electronic charges for Artesunate.

The blue in color occurs on all the ketonic oxygens and acidic oxygen in 4-methoxy-4oxobutanic acid group, and oxygen in isochromene, these are electrons rich sites, so electrophiles can easily attack these sites. The red in color occurs in the methyl carbons in the oxepane group, which is an electron-poor site, so nucleophiles can easily attack this site.
Molecular electrostatic potentials from nuclear charges for Artesunate.

From the blue in color to red in color as the numerical values between 0.00 and 50.00, and the molecule within ±15.65 Bohr3 range. The nuclear charge between 25.00 and 30.00, green in color on all the hydrogen atoms in the whole molecule denote positive electrostatic potentials and repulsions between protons and nuclei., and these sites mostly undergo substitution reactions possible, the nuclear charge between 45.00 and 50.00, red in color denotes negative electrostatic potentials and strong attractions between protons and core and lone pair of electrons in heavy atoms like oxygen and carbon atoms in the whole molecule, and these sites mostly undergo addition reactions.
Average localized ionization energy for Artesunate.

The greenish-blue color indicates the delocalized electrons occur on carbonyl oxygen‑carbon‑oxygen‑hydrogen in 4-methoxy-4-oxobutanoic acid, carbonyl oxygen‑carbon‑carbon in oxepane, and in isochromene groups. The blue color indicate the sigma bond between protons and carbons in 4-methoy-4oxobutanoic acid, and in isochromene groups.
Non-covalent interactions for Artesunate.

The hydrogen bond occurs the range from −0.16*10−1 to −0.05*10−1 a.u. between oxygen and methyl carbon protons in oxepane group, van der Waals force occurs the range from −0.01*10−1 to −0.04*10−1 a.u between carbonyl carbons and protons in 4-methoy-4-oxobutanoic acid group and steric interactions occurs the range from 0.03*10−1 to 0.22*10−1 a.u. between methyl groups and oxygen in isochromene group.
Localized orbital locator for Artesunate.

From the blue in color to red in color as the numerical values between 0.00 and 0.80, and the molecule within −15.65 to 17.36 Bohr3 range. The blue color range between 0.00 and 0.20 denotes strong pi-delocalized orbital occur in isochromen- ring, valence orbitals on carbon and oxygen atoms, and all the hydrogen atoms in the molecule. The color red range between 0.680 and 0.800 denotes weak pi-delocalized orbitals between hydrogen and carbon, carbon and carbon, carbon and oxygen, and core orbitals in artesunate.
Local information entropy for Arteunate.

From the blue in color to red in color as the numerical values between 0.00 and 1.00, and the molecule within ±15.65 Bohr3 range. All the hydrogen and some carbon atoms in artesunate molecule having very low local information entropy values between 0.00 and 0.02, which means this molecule is more stable with low uncertainty electrons in spatial distribution and blue in color depends upon masses of hydrogen, and carbon atoms. The greenish-blue color range between 0.55*10−1 and 0.65*10−1 denotes moderated entropy values on oxygen and some carbon atoms in the molecule, having moderated uncertainty in nature.
Reduced density gradient for Artesunate.

From the blue in color to red in color as the numerical values between 0.00 and 1.00, and the molecule within ±15.65 Bohr3 range. The red in color show the most probability of high electronic reduced density between 0.90 and 1.00, and greenish-yellow color range between 0.65 and 0.75 shows moderated electronic reduced density on carbon and oxygen atoms and 4-methoxy-4-oxobutanoic acid, isochromen-, and pyran groups in the molecule show in Fig. 10.
Frontier molecular orbital studies of Artesunate.
Adsorption energies of Artesunate over the GQD.
Non-linear optics property for Artesunate.

row.entry: Comparison of Artesunate with urea and p-nitroacetanilide
1.43 times lesser urea and 2.54 times PNA
1.13 times lesser urea and 10.32 times PNA
9.08 times greater urea and 1.80 times PNA
8.79 times greater urea and 1.35 times PNA
9.08 times greater urea and 1.80 times PNA

head.ce:title: Structural aspects, conformational preference and other physico-chemical properties of Artesunate and the formation of self-assembly with graphene quantum dots: A first principle analysis and surface enhancement of Raman activity investigation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, St. Berchmans College (Autonomous), Changanassery, Kerala, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, St. Berchmans College (Autonomous)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, St. Berchmans College (Autonomous), Changanassery, Kerala, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, College of Science, King Saud University, P.O. Box 2455, Riyadh 11451, Saudi Arabia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Science, King Saud University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, College of Science, King Saud University, P.O. Box 2455, Riyadh 11451, Saudi Arabia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, College of Science, Ibb University, P. O. Box 70270, Ibb, Yemen
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, College of Science, Ibb University, P. O. Box 70270, Ibb, Yemen

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Artesunate and its derivatives belong to a family of compounds that are highly effective against Malaria. This drug is active against several other conditions like neurodegerative diseases and cancer. The compound forms a metabolite dihydroartemisinine, which contain an endoperoxide bridge that is stimulated by heme iron leading to oxidative stress, protein and nucleic acid synthesis inhibition, morphologic changes and reduced growth and survival of parasites. This manuscript examines the structure and conformational preferences of the compound and investigates the energy and reactivity descriptors using computational simulations. Studies indicates that the compound forms stable self-assemblies with graphene quantum dots and modified graphene quantum dots, where the more stable complex was formed with the modified system. This self-assembly formation leads to enhancement in Raman activity of the compound after complex formation, hence can be used an analytical tool for the detection of the compound in a mixture. Natural bond orbital analysis examines the various intramolecular electron delocalisations. This drug is found to be a nonlinear optically active compound with respect to standards materials like urea and paranitroacetanilide. We also report the non covalent interactions present in the molecule, reduced density gradient, Lapalcian electron density and local information entropy of the molecule.

ce:section.ce:para: Artesunate is a medicine developed for the control of one of the most deadly protozoan disease Malaria. It can also be used for the management of post infection anaemia among children [1]. It is a water soluble semisynthetic analogue of a lactone artemisin with and anti-malarial, anti schistosomiasis and anti-viral properties. A recent study reported in 2019 indicated that the compound show anticancer properties also by regulating the heme synthesis in the blood [2]. Also, this molecule is found to be effective in the management of several CNS issues like bacterial infection, haemorrhage, ischemic cerebrovascular disorders and tumours of the nervous system [3]. Hence, this can be considered as a magic drug, capable of modifying several brain related illnesses. Artemisinine and aretesunate is rapidly metabolised into the active metabolite dihydroartemisinine (DHA), which contain an endoperoxide bridge that is stimulates by heme iron leading to oxidative stress, protein and nucleic acid synthesis inhibition, morphologic changes and reduced growth and survival of parasites. Reactions with these free radicals can also lead to alkylation of parasitic proteins such as a calcium adenosine triphosphatase and EXP1, a glutathione S-transferase [4]. Artesunate and DHA, are active against the blood-stage asexual parasites and gametocytes of Plasmodium species and chloroquine resistant strains. The other related anti-malarials from this family are artemether-lumefantrine (AL) [5], artesunate-amodiaquine (ASAQ) [6], and dihydroartemisinin-piperaquine (DP) [7,8].

Computational chemistry is a very important tool for study of molecules; its structure, topology and various physico-chemical properties of molecules [9–14]. Here, we use different techniques of the density functional theory to predict and analyse the molecular properties of the drug molecule artesumate. A detailed survey of the literature suggested that no serious attempt has been reported till now to study the structure and molecular properties of the compound using computational tools. Being a very important drug with a wide range of potential applications, such studies will help in the design of new derivatives and analogues with more effectiveness and less toxicity. Detection of the compound from a sample is also very important, as equal to that of the property investigation. We found that Artesunate can be detected using Surface Enhanced Raman Spectroscopy [15–18], which is a very useful tool for detection. Also, we report the study of noncovalent interactions, other global descriptors like average local ionization energy, local information entropy, molecular electrostatic potential etc. The compound is found to form stable self-assembly complexes with graphene quantum dots and amine decorated/modified quantum dots.

Artesunate molecule was optimized using Gaussian-09 software [19] and package using the hybrid PBEPBE functional [20] and aug-cc-pVDZ basis set [21–24]. Default settings were used for the SCF convergence of the optimisation step. We performed the frequency calculations to ensure that there exists no imaginary frequency such that the obtained geometry corresponds to a global minimum. Conformational dynamics of the molecule was studied using potential energy scan. The Raman spectra were also simulated in the same level of theory. The optimized geometry was used to predict the other local and global properties of the molecule. The excited state properties of the compound was studied using time dependent density functional theory TD-DFT using long range correction incorporated CAM-B3LYP functional [25] with same basis set. Wavefunction file generated from the above optimized geometry was used in Multiwavefunction software [26] to study the topological properties of the drug. Surface Enhancement of Raman Spectra was investigated after constructing a complex of the compound with graphene quantum dot and a amine modified graphene quantum dot. Energy values indicates that the complex is stable. The comparison of the intensity of the Raman spectra of the compound, molecular host and the complex was made to check for enhancement after scaling.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structure and conformational preference of the compound
ce:section.ce:para: Artesunate molecular structure was optimized by using density functional theory method for structural confirmation, DFT-B3LYP as a method, and aug-cc-pVDZ as a basis set. The optimized structure for artesunate shows in Fig. 1 .

Potential energy scan was employed to study the conformational preference of the compound. Detailed relaxed scan about the dihedrals of the artesumate along the bonds C24- C25-C26-C27 was carried out at the interval of 10 degrees in 36 steps using PBEPBE/cc-pVDZ level of theory. The global maxima is found to be at the 0 degree dihedral and global minima is found to be at 300 degrees. The relative conformational stabilisation energy is 0.40 eV or 9.22 kcal/mol with respect to the eclipsed conformation (See Fig. 2 ).

A powerful practical model for describing chemical reactivity is the frontier molecular orbital (FMO) theory, developed by Kenichi Fukui in 1950's. The important aspect of the frontier electron theory is the focus on the highest occupied and lowest unoccupied molecular orbitals (HOMO and LUMO) [27–31]. This helps to get an idea about the stability and reactivity of a molecule with the help of many other descriptors. For this compound, HOMO is −5.65 eV and LUMO is −1.93 eV. The band gap is 3.72 eV, which suggest that the compound is a molecular insulator. It may be due to the non-aromatic nature of the compound [32,33]. The relation between the descriptors and the FMO values are provided in the Table 1 . Ionization energy of the compound is 5.65 eV and electron affinity 1.93 eV [34,35]. The global hardness of the molecule is 1.86 and global softness is 0.59 [35–40]. The chemical potential, which is a descriptor of the stability of the molecule, is −3.79 eV. The electrophilicity index is high as that of 3.86, while the nucleophilicity index is low [41–43]. During the charge transfer, the fraction of charge exchanges is 2.04. It can be concluded that the compound is more electrophilic in nature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Molecular self-assembly formation of artesunate over graphene and modified graphene quantum dots and enhancement of Raman activities
ce:section.ce:para: Detection of the drug in a mixture of compounds is essential for the pharmaceutical assay of the compounds. Surface Enhanced Raman Spectra is widely used as a tool for this purpose [16,17,44–47]. It is found that the compound adsorbs well on the surface of graphene quantum dots (GQD) and amine modified graphene quantum dots (MGQD). Table 2 provides the adsorption energy of the drugs over the substrates. Adsorption energy of artesunate over GQD is 22.74 kcal/mol while that with MGQD is 24.66 kcal/mol (see Table 2). It is found that MGQD adsorbs preferentially more than that of virgin GQDs. It is may be due to the formation of additional non-covalent interactions between artesunate and amine modified GQD, due to the acidic nature of the former. Fig. 3a and b represents the location of the frontier orbitals of the complex and modified. In the case of the assembly with the drug and the virgin QD, the HOMO was found to be in the drug and the LUMO at the graphene. But due to the presence of electron rich amino group, both HOMO and LUMO was located over the amino decorated QD in the case of the second complex.

We observed significant enhancement of Raman activity of the compounds when they are adsorbed over grapheme and modified grapheme surfaces. This can be used as a good tool for the ultra-sensitive detection of the compounds in a mixture of several similar pharmaceutical agents [48]. In case of the complex formed by the adsorption of artesunate over G was found to be stable as the adsorption energy was found to be negative. At 3560 cm−1, that corresponds to the OH vibration of the -COOH group, an enhancement of almost 2 times in Raman activity can be observed for the adsorbed compound when compared to the virgin compound. Because of the CH stretching of artesunate, at 3042 cm−1, the enhancement factor was found to be 6, while at 1784 cm−1, that is due to the CO stretching of the -COOH group of artesunate, almost 2 times of enhancement in Raman activity can be found. The Raman and the SERS spectra of artesunate, G and the artesunate-G complex is shown at Fig. S1.

In case of the complex formed by the adsorption of artesunate over the surface of graphene amine, enhancements of Raman activity can be seen. At 3065 cm−1, that corresponds to the CH stretching of artesunate, the enhancement factor was found to be slightly more than 2. At 1785 cm−1 and 1751 cm−1, that correspond to the CO vibrations of the compound, the enhancement factors were seen to be 2 and 21.6 respectively. Fig. S2 depicts the Raman and SERS spectra of artesunate, graphene amine and the artesunate-graphene amine complex.

Non-linear optical activity is the response of the towards light by non-centrosymmetric molecules and tis find immense applications in the design of new organic electronic materials. Polarisation and hyperpolarisation data obtained during the Raman Spectra simulation can help in this regard. The Raman spectra of this compound was simulated in the same level of theory used for optimisation of the molecule. The observed non-linear optical property for artesunate is given in Table.3 . We compared it with the standard NLO materials urea and para-nitroacetanlide [49,50]. Artesunate having dipole moment (μ) is 2.10 D, which is 1.43 times lesser urea and 2.54 times greater than p-nitro acetanilide, hyperpolarizability (β) is 78.16*10−37 esu, which is 1.12 times lesser urea and 10.31 times lesser than p-nitro acetanilide, mean polarizability (α0) is 220.58*10−23 esu, which is 9.07 times greater urea and 1.80 times greater than p-nitro acetanilide, anisotropy of the polarizability (Δα) is 466.49*10−23 esu, which is 8.80 times greater urea and 1.35 times greater than p-nitro acetanilide, and molar refractivity (MR) is 5566.37 esu, which is 9.10 times greater urea and 1.79 times greater than p-nitro acetanilide (PNA) respectively [51,52].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Nature bonding orbital (NBO) property for Artesunate
ce:section.ce:para: We used Natural bond orbital analysis to study the various intramolecular interactions [23,53–59]. The NBO 3.1 suite [60] available with Gaussian 09 was used for this purpose. This study explains the electron delocalisation from bonding, core pair, lone pairs, and anti-bonding electrons in corresponding orbitals to anti-bonding, and anti-Rydberg orbitals.

The electron transition occurs from bonding orbitals to anti-bonding orbitals, are from σ (C10-H28), and σ (C16-H35) having the occupancies are 1.96, and 1.97 to σ* (C9-C13), and σ* (C18-C19) by taking the energies are 3.82 and 3.68 kcal/mol respectively, from σ (C19-H39) has the occupancy is 1.97 to σ* (O4-C18), and σ* (C10-C16) having the energies are 4.04, and 3.90 kcal/mol respectively, from σ (C19-H40), σ (C20-H41), σ (C23-H48), σ (C23-H49), σ (C23-H50), σ (C25-H51) and σ (C26-H53) with the occupancies are, 1.97, 1.974 1.98, 1.98, 1.97, 1.97, and 1.95 to σ* (O2-C18), σ* (O3-C13), σ* (O2-C18), σ* (C18-C19), σ* (O4-C18), σ* (O6-C24), and σ* (O8-C27) having the energies are 4.14, 3.73, 5.08, 4.10, 4.72, 3.96, and 5.65 kcal/mol respectively.

The electron transitions occur from core pair orbitals to anti-Rydberg orbitals. From CR (O6) and CR (O8) the same occupancy is 1.99 to R* (C24) and R* (C27) having the energies are 6.06 and 6.21 kcal/mol respectively.

The lone pair of electrons transition from corresponding orbitals to anti-bonding and anti-Rydberg orbitals. From n (O2) has the occupancy is 1.88 to σ* (O3-C13), σ* (O4-C18), and σ* (C18-C23) having the energies are 11.19, 6.47, and 5.37 kcal/mol respectively, from n (O3), has the occupancy is 1.96 to σ* (C9-C13), σ* (C15-C20), σ* (O5-C20), and σ* (O2-C13) having the energies are 4.54, 3.57, 15.49, and 12.05 kcal/mol respectively, from n (O4), has the occupancy is 1.93 to σ* (O2-C18) with the energy is 13.28 kcal/mol, from n (O5) has the occupancy is 1.76 to σ* (O3-C20), and σ* (O6-C24) having the energies are 10.03, and 37.31 kcal/mol respectively, from n (O7), and n (O8) with the occupancies are 1.81 and 1.98 to σ* (O8-C27), and R* (C27) having the energies are 37.36, and 13.34 kcal/mol respectively, from n (O6), has the occupancy is 1.9763 to R* (C24), σ* (O5-C24), and σ* (C24-C25) having the energies are 13.23, 29.75, and 14.83 kcal/mol respectively, and from n(O8) has the occupancy is 1.84 to σ* (O7-C27), and σ* (C26-C27) having the energies are 31.46 and 14.75 kcal/mol respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Molecular electrostatic potentials (MESP) from electronic and nuclear charges
ce:section.ce:para: The electrostatic potential V(r) generated around a molecule by its nuclei and electrons which are treated as static charge distribution is a very useful property for studying and predicting molecular reactive actions. This is narrowly defined and can be calculated both experimentally and computationally [61–63]. The capacity has been especially useful as an indication of the positions or regions of the molecule to which the advancing electrophile is initially drawn, and has also been effectively extended to the analysis of associations requiring a certain optimal relative orientation of the reactants, such as between the product and its cellular receptor. Artesunate molecular structure was optimized by using density functional theory method for structural confirmation, DFT-B3LYP as a method, and aug-cc-pVDZ as a basis set, after that generated wavefunction file. This study explains the active sites occur in the molecule. Fig. 4 shows the reactivity sites for artesunate, the range between −15.57 and 20.80 Bohr3, from the color blue to red having the numerical value from −0.10 to 0.10.
The electrostatic potentials from nuclear charges for artesunate show in Fig. 5 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Average localized ionization energy (ALIE) property for Artesunate
ce:section.ce:para: Artesunate molecular structure was optimized by using density functional theory method for structural confirmation, DFT-B3LYP as a method, and aug-cc-pVDZ as a basis set, after that generated wavefunction file [64,65]. This study explains the localized and delocalized electrons can give stability and bioactivity increasing in the molecule. Fig. 6 shows the reactivity sites for artesunate, the range from −15.57 to 20.70 Bohr3, from the color blue to red having the numerical value from 0.00 to 2.00.

Artesunate molecular structure was optimized by using density functional theory method for structural confirmation, DFT-B3LYP as a method, and aug-cc-pVDZ as a basis set, after that generated wavefunction file [66,67]. This study explains the non-bond interactions and bioactivity for artesunate in Fig. 7 , which is a graph plotted energy against reduced density gradient.

This study explains further the electronic structure for artesunate. The higher value of electron localization function is strongly localized and low value is strong delocalization of electron in this molecule. ELF can be used to distinguish between a chemical bond and a comparatively weak chemical bond [68].The electron localized function (ELF) for artesunate shown in Fig. S3.

From the blue in color to red in color as the numerical values between 0.00 and 1.00, and the molecule within −15.65 to 17.36 Bohr3 range. The color red range between 0.85 and 1.00 indicates most probability of localized pi-electrons appears on all the hydrogen atomsattached with carbon and oxygen atoms, and core electrons in heavy atoms like carbon and hydrogen atoms in the molecule, lone pair of electrons in oxygens and nitrogens in the whole molecule shows strongly localized electrons. Moderated delocalized electrons show by green in color appears in whole rings, and chain in the molecule. The blue in color between the range 0.00 and 0.20 denotes strong delocalized electrons in the molecule in isochromen- ring, and lone pair of electrons and valence electrons in heavy atoms like oxygen and carbon in the molecule.
This study explains the localized and delocalized molecular orbitals locations for artesunate [69–71]. The localized orbital locator (LOL) for artesunate shown in Fig. 8 .

LIE study explains the stability and reactivity of the molecule [72–75]. Entropy is a feature of probability distributions and can take to be a qualification of uncertainty. The high value of local information entropy is directionally proportional to the uncertainty of electrons in spatial distribution. Fig. 9 shows local information entropy for artesunate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reduced density gradient (RDG) for Artesunate and Laplacian of electron density for Artesunate
ce:section.ce:para: The reduced density gradient is directly proportional to the electronic density of the molecule, which means a small reduced density gradient is low electronic density [76,77]. Fig. 10 shows the reduced density gradient for artesunate.

The Laplace distribution, also called the double exponential distribution, is this distribution of differences between two independent variants with identical exponential distributions [78]. Fig. S4 shows the Laplace of electron density for artesunate.

From the blue in color to red in color as the numerical values between −8.00 and 15.00, and the molecule within −13.82 to 15.65 Bohr3 range. The color red range between 12.70 and 15.00 shows high flexible density occurs on heavy atoms like carbon and oxygen atoms, and the blue color range between −8.00 and − 3.40 show low flexible density occurs on hydrogen and carbon atoms in the artesunate molecule.

The structure and conformational analysis of the molecule is reported in the ground state. Energy descriptor analysis gives insights on the reactivity and stability properties of the molecule. It shows that the compound is inherently stable and bioactive. Polarisability and hyperpolarisability study indicates that the compound is less high when compared to the standard materials urea and para-nitroacteanilide. The compound also showed very high molar refractivity. NBO studies shows that the compound is inherently stable due to several intramolecular electron delocalisations. The adsorption indicates that artesumnate forms stable complex with graphene and decorated graphene quantum dots and this property was used to detect the presence of this compound in a mixture of compounds using SERS. The reaction site properties were identified using electrostatic potentials, average localized ionization energy, and noncovalent interactions and found that 4-methoxy-4-oxobutanoic acid, pyran, oxepane/isochromene group in artesunate are more prone to chemical and biological reactions. This molecule and their derivatives are suitable candidates to the future study for antiviral activities against the new COVID-19 viruses due to their already demonstrated anti-viral activity.

T. Pooventhiran: Investigation, Writing-original draft, editing. Nabil Alzaqri: Resources, Funding acquisition, editing, Validation. Utsab Bhattacharyya: Methodology, validation, writing- review, editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: Authors declare no conflicts of interest

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors extend their appreciation to the Deanship of Scientific Research, King Saud University for funding this work through research group no (RG-1440-141).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Post-malarial anemia in Mozambican children treated with quinine or artesunate: a retrospective observational study

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Varo, L. Quintó, A. Sitoe, L. Madrid, S. Acácio, P. Vitorino, A.M. Valente, A. Mayor, D. Camprubí, J. Muñoz, G. Bambo, E. Macete, C. Menéndez, P.L. Alonso, P. Aide, Q. Bassat, Post-malarial anemia in Mozambican children treated with quinine or artesunate: A retrospective observational study, Int. J. Infect. Dis. 96 (2020) 655–662. doi:10.1016/j.ijid.2020.05.089.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Synergistic antitumor activity of artesunate and HDAC inhibitors through elevating heme synthesis via synergistic upregulation of ALAS1 expression

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C.-P. Chen, K. Chen, Z. Feng, X. Wen, H. Sun, Synergistic antitumor activity of artesunate and HDAC inhibitors through elevating heme synthesis via synergistic upregulation of ALAS1 expression, Acta Pharm. Sin. B. 9 (2019) 937–951. doi:10.1016/j.apsb.2019.05.001.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The potential therapeutic effects of Artesunate on stroke and other central nervous system diseases

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Supergenomic network compression and the discovery of EXP1 as a glutathione Transferase inhibited by Artesunate
